speaker: interviewer
text: Four, Mr. Ignac Feldman. They mobilized you into the Russian Army in July of 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will never forget the date, because in my youth, I learned history. And  I knew the 14th of July was the day in Paris they stormed the Bastille fortress. So for that, this is my mind, the 14th of July, but this was 1942. I leave my wife-- that was the place where we live was maybe 75 or 80 homes-- Cossacks. In every home, somebody made from gloves, somebody made socks, and give something for me, because they told me I am their man. I work hard. And they liked me. They liked my wife, even with the war. But what my wife needed for her or the child to eat, she every time and got it. But I come to the front again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you come to the front, tell me about the trip. What did you see as you were traveling? Did you see--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was mobilized. And I went. Yes, there was also a friend with me. You see, he was also mobilized. But he was in the other direction. And I went the other direction. This was Tolovsky Josef. And I see once that  we come there in the gate. We must back up, because the German attacked and were on the offensive. And we were on the defensive. And then was-- it was very bad. And we was going, and the Russian front collapsed. On one morning, I heard the same command like I heard in 1939 then the German command to give up as war prisoners. I took away my gun-- my machine gun. But the anti-tank grenade I keep on me, under my coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Russian friend then asked me, Ignac, why did you do that? If they noticed, they will kill you. I don't know. They did not kill me.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they noticed, I would go where there was most concentrated Germans. And I would blow me up together with them. They cannot kill me-- I will kill them together with me. But there was an opening there that came straight near a forest. I escaped. Then I met other Russians. I met another high-ranking Russian officer. He noticed I had the anti-tank grenade. He says, oh, very good. And we started walking and hiding. Comes once a German patrol close, and two Russian officers. They took my hand grenade. And they can-- quieted them down. Then I thought, I know, I'll leave my wife there. And she only-- the-- I asked the last news what the officers tell me, what the Germans tell. Then I know one line they have to go, but where to go more to east, my wife. And though we come-- in a few days, I come very close to the place-- seven kilometers from the place where I leave my wife. And I was hiding-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] is high. And I look. And I see the Germans parade there in the place where I leave my wife, everything. And once I passed a Cossack on his horse, a middle-aged Cossack. I remember his name-- Radchenko. Then I call him, Radchenko. He couldn't see me. He'd look around. He stopped on his horse-- a horseman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I gave him the second command, Radchenko. Then he come closer. Then he come close, was afraid-- what are you doing here? I told him, listen, you know they took us around, so I escaped. I like to go to take my wife. And he told me your wife with the child is safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me, [NON-ENGLISH], Cossack. He evacuated what they can, to not fall in the German's hands. Your wife and the other-- your friend's wife is evacuated. And the manager tell, Mrs. Feldman cannot stay here because the Germans would kill her. Let's evacuate her on the place. It was very bad because I knew she had a child, and she was pregnant with the other child. These were the times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then he asked me, what can I do, Ignac, for you? tell him, I tell him, you know, Radchenko, I'd like from you something. But this is your life. How can I tell you to take your life? He told me, you're not [INAUDIBLE]. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask for your horse. For the horseman to ask for the horse is to take his life. I ask you the horse. And I stuck--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to jump over, you see, the German lines on the side roads. And I cannot walk anymore. I'm tired. Don't you know what you ask? I told him, yes, Radchenko, for that. What is your decision? I'm not forcing you. He  said, take it. You are like my brother. He gave me the horse. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was riding at night. It was quiet. The people are good to give me sometimes bread to eat. After 250 kilometers, the horse collapsed, because I cannot take care of the hooves. And I came very close to a city. Makhakchala is the name. And I knew accordingly what they told me is if my wife ever gets-- they have only one line-- is this to Makhakchala. But I don't know if she is there, or if she will came. Because she was going with a wagon-- with the very primitive. And I was with the horse, who was faster. Then, again, I have no documents. If the Russians catch me, they can kill me as a deserter. They can kill me because I have no documents. I am in uniform, but no documents-- a Russian uniform, but no documents. They could kill me as a deserter-- as a spy. Everything depends on what an officer would admit. Then I am hiding near the station a few hundred yards and to see maybe what is what. At once, I see a friend of hers, a young girl, she was singing in her [NON-ENGLISH], in [NON-ENGLISH] in the city of Lodz. Her name was Jadzia Epstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I called Jadzia. She was stunned. Who called her name? Jadzia Epstein, come close. She comes here. I said, listen, Jadzia. What are you doing? I told her my position is very bad-- no documents. And I escaped. Nobody is safe from this division. They can kill me like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife-- you know Lonia, little Lonia-- we her little Lonia-- she is with a child. I don't know if she is on the station or she will come. She can have a Polish document, a Russian document, a civil clothing to take away the uniform because I'm not a Soviet citizen and save my life. She said listen, you hide, and I will look for Lonia-- my wife. Maybe 20 minutes later, I am in hiding-- comes out the KGB-- your hand high with the bayonet. Your documents. I don't have no documents, I tell them.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where you came? I tell them-- in this and this division, I was. But nobody got out killed by the German war prisoners. And I escaped. They did not believe me. They arrested me and they put the commandant away. Later, I knew what this was happened with my wife, later. But when I was sitting on the commandant [INAUDIBLE]. Two days, only gave us a drink of water, because they keep deserters or spies. Then I gave them where I was born, which division I was. You are Polish? How did you came, everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then once command comes a colonel. And he gave the command-- [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], this is, they not give you the proper ammunition, and you must attack the Germans. You can go out on this, or you lose your head or your life. How long you are alive, you cannot go out from there. But I know the regulations are military. Then I step out, and I tell him. Then he tell me. And I tell him, I'm not a Russian citizen. Then he tell me to go in. I know he must give three times the order down here-- to load to shoot. Then I tell him, in Stalin's name this and this day, Stalin said, every Polish citizen is supposed to be-- go to the direction of the Polish army. If you kill me, this is against Stalin. You will be responsible. When I told him, then he would be against Stalin. He put the hand down. He told to the commander take him back to the jail. It was a saving.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came back to the jail. After two days, the commandant told me, listen, maybe you are right. But how can I transport you? You are alone. You will go on a battalion to work. OK, better than I thought-- I was working only-- a week and a half comes a special commission from Moscow that different people who were running away. Then they have my papers. They look, uh-huh, you are from Polish army, you are this and this. You are a specialist. You will be students.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they take us to make full the knowledge about the dynamite-- what they and others are supposed to explain. And after like in an auditorium and everything, they give us the uniform, and they took us to the front. And I was this-- we knew to take out mines. If the Russians army backed down, they were the last. We'll put them in their mines against the Germans. If the Russians attack, you must take out the Russian and the German mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all this. A little mistake for a second, no more life, because you can make a mistake once in life because if not, you are blown-- you're blowing up in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: In meantime, we come. We have to no idea where my wife is. The girl, Jadzia, defended us. She met my wife. And she said, listen, your [NON-ENGLISH] is here-- Ignac is here. But she cannot help. She is with the child with swollen legs, hanging down. She cannot help. And I was arrested on one side, she was on the other. And after she had other life, she goes further and with no connection. And this was going on. There was also-- always I know she is safe. Or she will survive alone in this condition with the child, maybe with two children-- I don't know. This comes till 1943. I have good friends on the front-- like you see in the picture, for certain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [NON-ENGLISH], Laschenko Denisov, one of the best friends, you see in the picture what I was telling you? When he write, he gave me another picture. Then later, I have all the connection with my wife, I sent it over to my wife. Then we came to Stalingrad. When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. We came to Stalingrad. And it just was a murderous-- a murderous fight. Winter-- minus 35, but OK, Good-- I have good boots, and good-- but it is still so cold. And we was laying a few hours. Nobody can keep up the head. Or even you must keep the legs like that-- if you keep the legs like that, shot down. It was like a fire from of sky-- like a hell. Never in mankind was there a fight like that. People was fighting from door to door-- from building to building on the street. It is-- is is like a hell on our planet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the last few hours, we were laying. And then we hear, and we can see the Germans got out with the white flags. And we hear they-- through the [NON-ENGLISH]-- how is that? Loudspeaker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Loudspeakers. [NON-ENGLISH]-- stop shooting, because it's finished. The Germans give up. And I like to-- to step-- to step out. I [INAUDIBLE] I don't feel my legs, because it was so cold. And he helped me right away, my friend. He called the [NON-ENGLISH] and they bring me over the river, over the Volga. And there was underground was the hospital, because they could not keep the hospital like that. And underground was the hospital with the electricity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then comes the nurse and she like to cut-- to take off my boots. Then comes the doctor, and stopped her-- don't do it. She said, why? Cut. Then she said, it is new boots. It is new, because you can pull down his skin together with the boots. Cut it. And she cut off the boots. She take it off.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had no penicillin. He comes with a pincer, and take out toenails from my legs. They was black. And he telled her, every 10 minutes take snow and put down to compress and to give the blood circulation. And I was there in the hospital. But after a while, after two weeks, they sent me again. I was feeling better. They sent me again to the front. Then I come to [NON-ENGLISH], the River Don is the river. Don is a big river and Donets is a little river that comes to the Don. And this, you call this Seversky, Donets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the front is staying-- you hear the German commanders. We hear our commanders. We know the Germans, they know our commanders they-- and sometimes you hear the German can call, stop shooting. We'd like to eat breakfast. And the front are staying. You see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what I like to go back to Stalingrad. I forgot a moment. It was so cold. In their attack, the Russians put a few divisions who comes from Siberia. And for them, 35 minus is like nothing. They took off their coats. They put like that-- the coat like that in a shield. And they attacked. The Germans cannot breathe even. You know, they put scarves-- they put what they can-- any matter around their neck. And when they would breathe, it was pieces of ice. And when they shoot, they can never shoot until it is going like at that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then they see the Siberikos from Siberia, the young people, just demolished them. And this was very, very important in [INAUDIBLE]. This is-- I'll forget to mention about that. But on the Seversky Donets, but when we-- after the order comes to attack, and we are attacked, we jump in, and the [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know what's [NON-ENGLISH]? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where the-- deep in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trenches?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trenches where the Germans were staying-- I see boxes of ammunition and whatnot jutting out was written on the boxes from the ammunition. [GERMAN]-- where's-- where's my home? [GERMAN] Would I see my home again?

speaker: interviewer
text: [GERMAN]-- how is this in English? In German, [GERMAN] How long they were going and attach and to kill was good. But at the moment, they saw what is the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then also I was, you see, with the army at once-- I don't have, to Red Cross answer me, and they didn't where my wife with the child is-- to Moscow, the Red Cross. They don't know. They don't have the statistic where she is. And from the place of the [NON-ENGLISH], where I leave my wife, was liberated. I send a letter to them. In the meantime, my wife was so smart, she sent also a letter. And I know one Cossack family-- the wife is literate. The other are illiterate-- she can write. And when I wrote the letter, and I asked her, maybe you remember my wife with my child. I am in the army. I have no connection. If you know, your husband, he evacuate my wife and the other woman, please let me know. She wrote a letter back. And she told me like that, I am writing to you with tears in my eyes. Remember the Cossack tradition. If you were to stay alive from where you go out in fight like that, you come back, it is your home. Your wife is secure. And she sent my letter to my wife, because my wife sent-- in my wife's letter to me, but took three months till I received the letter. And then see the other friends tell us they are together in the city of Bukhara. This means after three years, I didn't know what is what. Then when I was gunned down here, and here. And I was thinking, OK, I cannot go now back on the front. Maybe they will give me free to go to see my wife. I haven't seen her so long-- three years. I talked with the doctor. The doctor said, you know, you are Jewish. [INAUDIBLE] the manager from the hospital is also a Jewish woman, Maria Mazevna. You talk it over with Maria Mazevna, and maybe you will go. Then I talk over Maria Mazevna, she looked at me. She started talking a little bit Jewish to me. [YIDDISH], she say-- she tell me to listen. I will   not answer you, Ignac. I give you five minutes. When I go out, you have papers filling on the top drawer. Open the drawer in five minutes and look at what is written. She walked out. I opened the door to see what is written in Russian-- to take good care of me, and they need me on the front. And soon when I can go, even to supply room with a doctor and to bring me back to the battalion, with a doctor if I need. They need me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Stalingrad still?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this after Stalingrad. This a little bit-- a year later. This was already, you see, in-- And so was it-- when she comes back. She said, OK, I know she can nothing do, because she had the order, with the talking, because when she will show me she can be go to jail herself, and not talking-- good friends. We said goodbye to each other. And then I was still on the-- Meantime, I was back to my battalion. And at once, we take a high ranking German officer, you see, and several prisoners. We attacked them, and we took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I was supposed to question him. And I asked him a few questions. Then he asked me the questions. [GERMAN]? Is this not a shame when a German asks a high-ranking German? I told him-- I am not German. I am Jewish. Then he gave the right answer and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then was till we come in Czechoslovakia. Czechoslovakia-- we to to Hungary and to Slovakia. There is a difference between the Slovaks and the Czechs. On every home was the slogan they didn't have time to take off. [NON-ENGLISH]-- We will clear Slovakia from Jews. [NON-ENGLISH], this is the quislings from Slovakia. You didn't meet-- you didn't see Jewish population. You see what has happened. You know what has happened. Then we came to Romania. So I passed in the Carpathian Mountains to Romania. And I saw the first city. Meantime, before you come to Romania, you pass [NON-ENGLISH]-- there was a fortress. And the Germans went down in the fortress in the mountains. And we have the order to blow them out-- to concentrate two tons dynamite, and to blow them up. Because they had to live. And they were shooting. They was going in-- they cannot [INAUDIBLE]. The Russian comes out to go, they start shooting at them. And then they hide-- and they must kill them. And then when we blow up-- blow up the two-ton dynamite with a smoke. I, with a lieutenant, there was as the picture, was going even like that with the a shield. It was so hot and everything. And this picture, I don't have. But I have other picture with this Laschenko, only this. And for that, I have a personally thank you from Generalissimus Stalin as taking part to break the German line on this-- in this front part. I know I was a small school in a giant Russian war machine. It was a school he sent. I have other medals. I have medals, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Bratislva, [NON-ENGLISH]-- maybe 10 medals. I never took out the medals. I have only the document, because I, again, in my nature against uniforms, but the document to give us. And then the thankfulness from the-- from his signature, in the Generalissimus Stalin. And then I have the connection with my wife after three years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Please.
